title: Why Producers Can’t Wait: Dynamic Capital Allocation
body_html:
The consumer can sometimes wait. The producer often cannot. That is one of the essay’s best counters to the standard deflation panic. A household might postpone a discretionary purchase because prices are likely to be lower next year. But a producer faces rivals, and rivals compress the value of waiting.

If a key input is getting cheaper because someone upstream is innovating, the producer’s real problem is not whether an even better deal might arrive later. The real problem is whether a competitor will buy first, cut prices first, improve his own returns first, and leave everyone else with a weaker capital base. In competitive markets, delay is often more expensive than action.

This is especially true in capital goods and technology. Better machines, tools, and software are not passive consumer temptations; they are strategic weapons. Buying them early can improve quality, throughput, service, or cost structure enough to fund the next upgrade. That iterative compounding is exactly how deflationary sectors keep moving.

So the image of a producer frozen by the prospect of lower future prices misunderstands how competition works. In real markets, the pressure to stay in motion usually dominates the temptation to wait.
